original_abstract: Abstract Research on different machine learning (ML) has become incredibly popular during the past few decades. However, for some researchers not familiar with statistics, it might be difficult to understand how to evaluate the performance of ML models and compare them with each other. Here, we introduce the most common evaluation metrics used for the typical supervised ML tasks including binary, multi-class, and multi-label classification, regression, image segmentation, object detection, and information retrieval. We explain how to choose a suitable statistical test for comparing models, how to obtain enough values of the metric for testing, and how to perform the test and interpret its results. We also present a few practical examples about comparing convolutional neural networks used to classify X-rays with different lung infections and detect cancer tumors in positron emission tomography images.
humanized_abstract: Abstract Even though the research of machine learning (ML) algorithms has received a high degree of attention in the literature in the last few decades, many researchers (especially those that are not so familiar with the basic statistics concepts) do not know how to calculate performance metrics for various supervised learning algorithms such as: - binary, multi-class and multi-label classification - regression - image segmentation - object detection - information retrieval And how to choose the appropriate statistical test for comparing machine learning models or algorithms and what are the key parameters and values that need to be calculated and known to perform the appropriate statistical tests, how to obtain these metrics, how to apply the statistical test and the interpretation of its results, all of these and more examples will be demonstrated in this tutorial, specifically with the comparison and evaluation of some deep learning convolutional neural networks (CNNs) that are used to classify X-Ray images infected with different lung diseases, as well as detecting cancer tumors in positron emission tomography (PET) images.
